rules.rule: Priority Review designation provides a shorter clock for review of the marketing application: 6 months compared with the 10-month standard review. It applies to applications for drugs that treat a serious condition and would provide a significant improvement in safety or effectiveness.
rules.verbatim_quote: Features: Shorter clock for review of marketing application (6 months compared with the 10-month standard review). Qualifying criteria: An application (original or efficacy supplement) for a drug that treats a serious condition AND, if approved, would provide a significant improvement in safety or effectiveness OR Any supplement that proposes a labeling change pursuant to a report on a pediatric study under 505A.
rules.fda_notes: Priority Review reduces the FDA review period from 10 to 6 months from the date of application filing. The designation is determined at the time of BLA/NDA filing, not during development.

rules.rule: For Breakthrough Therapy designation, a clinically significant endpoint is one that measures an effect on irreversible morbidity or mortality (IMM) or on symptoms that represent serious consequences of the disease. Substantial improvement is a matter of judgment based on both the magnitude of the drug's effect and the importance of the effect to the treatment of the serious condition.
rules.source_section: Section VI.A.4 - Substantial Improvement on Clinically Significant Endpoints
rules.verbatim_quote: Substantial Improvement: The determination of whether the improvement over available therapy is substantial is a matter of judgment and depends on both the magnitude of the drug's effect on a clinically significant endpoint (which could include duration of the effect) and the importance of the observed effect to the treatment of the serious condition or serious aspect of the condition... Clinically Significant Endpoint: For purposes of breakthrough therapy designation, FDA considers clinically significant endpoint generally to refer to an endpoint that measures an effect on irreversible morbidity or mortality (IMM) or on symptoms that represent serious consequences of the disease.
rules.fda_notes: FDA's clinically significant endpoint definition includes: effects on established surrogate endpoints (for traditional approval), effects on surrogate or intermediate endpoints for accelerated approval, and significantly improved safety profiles with similar efficacy.

rules.rule: An unmet medical need is a condition whose treatment or diagnosis is not addressed adequately by available therapy, including an immediate need for a defined population (no or limited treatment) or a longer-term need for society (e.g., to address the development of resistance to antibacterial drugs).
rules.verbatim_quote: An unmet medical need is a condition whose treatment or diagnosis is not addressed adequately by available therapy. An unmet medical need includes an immediate need for a defined population (i.e., to treat a serious condition with no or limited treatment) or a longer-term need for society (e.g., to address the development of resistance to antibacterial drugs).
rules.fda_notes: When available therapy exists, unmet medical need may be demonstrated by: improved effect on a serious outcome not addressed by available therapy, improved effect over available therapy, effect in patients unable to tolerate available therapy, effect in patients who failed available therapy, or improved safety with comparable efficacy.

fda_endpoints.actual_document_title: FDA Guidance for Industry - Clinical Trial Endpoints for the Approval of Cancer Drugs and Biologics, CDER/CBER/Oncology Center of Excellence, December 2018

rules.rule: Overall survival is defined as the time from randomization until death from any cause and is measured in the intent-to-treat population. Survival is considered the most reliable cancer endpoint and, when studies can be conducted to adequately assess survival, it is usually the preferred endpoint.
rules.verbatim_quote: Overall survival is defined as the time from randomization until death from any cause and is measured in the intent-to-treat population. Survival is considered the most reliable cancer endpoint, and when studies can be conducted to adequately assess survival, it is usually the preferred endpoint. This endpoint is precise and easy to measure, documented by the date of death. Bias is not a factor in endpoint measurement. Survival improvement should be analyzed as a risk-benefit analysis to assess clinical benefit.
rules.fda_notes: OS requires randomized controlled trials to be reliable. Data from externally controlled trials are seldom reliable for OS. A statistically significant improvement in OS with an acceptable toxicity profile has often supported new drug approval.

rules.rule: ORR is defined as the proportion of patients with tumor size reduction of a predefined amount and for a minimum time period. FDA has generally defined ORR as the sum of partial responses plus CRs. Stable disease should not be a component of ORR.
rules.verbatim_quote: ORR is defined as the proportion of patients with tumor size reduction of a predefined amount and for a minimum time period. Response duration usually is measured from the time of initial response until documented tumor progression. Generally, the FDA has defined ORR as the sum of partial responses plus CRs.
rules.fda_notes: Stable disease should NOT be a component of ORR per this guidance. Standardized criteria (e.g., RECIST 1.1) should be used to ascertain response. ORR can serve as a traditional approval endpoint, surrogate for accelerated approval, or surrogate for traditional approval in select contexts.

rules.rule: DFS is defined as the time from randomization until disease recurrence or death from any cause. EFS is defined as time from randomization to any of the following events: progression of disease that precludes surgery, local or distant recurrence, or death due to any cause.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.1 - Disease-Free Survival and Event-Free Survival
rules.verbatim_quote: Generally, DFS is defined as the time from randomization until disease recurrence or death from any cause. The most frequent use of this endpoint is in the adjuvant setting after definitive surgery or radiotherapy... For the purpose of this guidance, EFS is defined as time from randomization to any of the following events: progression of disease that precludes surgery, local or distant recurrence, or death due to any cause.
rules.fda_notes: DFS has been the primary basis of approval for adjuvant breast cancer hormonal therapy, adjuvant colon cancer, and adjuvant cytotoxic breast cancer therapy. EFS is appropriate for evaluation of neoadjuvant therapy.

rules.rule: PFS is defined as the time from randomization until objective tumor progression or death, whichever occurs first. TTP does not include deaths. Compared with TTP, PFS is the preferred regulatory endpoint because PFS includes deaths and thus can be a better correlate to overall survival.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.4 - Time to Progression and Progression-Free Survival
rules.verbatim_quote: TTP is defined as the time from randomization until objective tumor progression; TTP does not include deaths. PFS is defined as the time from randomization until objective tumor progression or death, whichever occurs first... Compared with TTP, PFS is the preferred regulatory endpoint. PFS includes deaths and thus can be a better correlate to overall survival.
rules.fda_notes: PFS data collection should be limited to specified time intervals around scheduled visits to minimize bias. Plans for PFS data collection and analysis should be discussed with FDA at end-of-phase 2 meetings.

rules.rule: Time to treatment failure (TTF) is defined as a composite endpoint measuring time from randomization to discontinuation of treatment for any reason, including disease progression, treatment toxicity, and death. TTF is generally not recommended as a regulatory endpoint for new molecular entity drug approval.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.5 - Time to Treatment Failure
rules.verbatim_quote: Time to treatment failure (TTF) is defined as a composite endpoint measuring time from randomization to discontinuation of treatment for any reason, including disease progression, treatment toxicity, and death. TTF is generally not recommended as a regulatory endpoint for new molecular entity drug approval.
rules.fda_notes: TTF conflates disease progression with tolerability. Since it incorporates treatment discontinuation for toxicity (a drug effect), it is considered biased and not a clean measure of clinical benefit.

rules.rule: CR is defined as no detectable evidence of tumor, generally measured through imaging studies (e.g., CT scans) or histopathologic assessment. CR has supported traditional approval in hematologic malignancies (e.g., leukemia) and pathologic CR in high-risk early-stage breast cancer as a surrogate endpoint for accelerated approval.
rules.verbatim_quote: CR is defined as no detectable evidence of tumor. CR is generally measured through imaging studies (e.g., CT scans) or through histopathologic assessment (e.g., bone marrow biopsy or breast cancer resection specimens). Treatment effect measured by CR can be a surrogate endpoint to support accelerated approval, a surrogate endpoint to support traditional approval, or it can represent direct clinical benefit based on the specific disease, context of use, magnitude of the effect, effect duration, disease setting, location of disease, available therapy, and the risk-benefit relationship.
rules.fda_notes: For hematologic malignancies such as acute leukemia, CR has been used as a clinical endpoint for traditional approval. For early high-risk breast cancer, pathologic CR (pCR) has been used as a surrogate endpoint for accelerated approval.

rules.rule: For drugs granted accelerated approval, postmarketing confirmatory trials have been required to verify and describe the anticipated effect on IMM or other clinical benefit. If the postmarketing studies fail to demonstrate clinical benefit or if the applicant does not demonstrate due diligence, FDA may withdraw approval.
rules.source_section: Section VII.D - Conditions of Accelerated Approval
rules.verbatim_quote: For drugs granted accelerated approval, postmarketing confirmatory trials have been required to verify and describe the anticipated effect on IMM or other clinical benefit. A drug is approved under the accelerated approval regulations on condition that the manufacturer conducts clinical studies to verify and describe the actual clinical benefit. If the postmarketing studies fail to demonstrate clinical benefit or if the applicant does not demonstrate due diligence in conducting the required studies, among other reasons, FDA may withdraw approval of the drug or indication.
rules.fda_notes: Confirmatory trials should usually be already underway at the time of accelerated approval. FDA tracks post-approval study commitments and may take action (including expedited withdrawal under FDASIA) if studies are not completed with due diligence.

rules.rule: When the primary study endpoint is based on tumor measurements (e.g., PFS or ORR), tumor assessments generally should be verified by central reviewers blinded to study treatments. An independent endpoint review committee (IRC) charter should be in place before initiation of the study.
rules.source_section: Section III.B / Appendix - Independent Review of Tumor Endpoints
rules.verbatim_quote: When the primary study endpoint is based on tumor measurements (e.g., PFS or ORR), tumor assessments generally should be verified by central reviewers blinded to study treatments (see Appendix) to ascertain lack of assessment bias... An independent endpoint review committee (IRC) can minimize bias in radiographic interpretation of the radiological findings and independent adjudication of assessments. A clearly written plan of the charter outlining the IRC function and process (independent review charter) should be in place before initiation of the study.
rules.fda_notes: Independent blinded review is not always required in blinded trials when effect sizes are robust. FDA may accept an audit-based approach (random sample of images reviewed centrally) with a detailed auditing plan.

rules.rule: Per 21 CFR 314.126, FDA approves drugs based on substantial evidence of efficacy from adequate and well-controlled investigations. Studies must allow a valid comparison to a control and must provide a quantitative assessment of the drug's effect. In many cases, at least two adequate and well-controlled clinical investigations are needed.
rules.source_section: Section IV - Clinical Trial Design and Analysis Considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: Per 21 CFR 314.126, the FDA approves drugs based on substantial evidence of efficacy from 'adequate and well-controlled investigations,' as described in the regulations. Studies must allow a valid comparison to a control and must provide a quantitative assessment of the drug's effect. The most reliable method for demonstrating efficacy is to show a statistically significant improvement in a clinically meaningful endpoint in randomized controlled trials (i.e., superiority).
rules.fda_notes: The 'two study rule' has exceptions. A single multinational trial providing highly reliable and statistically strong evidence of an important clinical benefit may suffice. For cancer drugs, evidence from one adequate and well-controlled study can support efficacy supplements for the same cancer at a different stage.

rules.rule: Surrogate endpoints for accelerated approval must be reasonably likely to predict clinical benefit per FD&C Act section 506(c)(1)(A), 21 CFR part 314 subpart H, and 21 CFR part 601 subpart E. ORR has been the most commonly used surrogate endpoint in support of accelerated approval in oncology.
rules.source_section: Section II.B - Endpoints Supporting Past Approvals
rules.verbatim_quote: Surrogate endpoints for accelerated approval must be reasonably likely to predict clinical benefit (FD&C Act § 506(c)(1)(A); 21 CFR part 314, subpart H; and 21 CFR part 601, subpart E). While durable ORR has been used as a traditional approval endpoint in some circumstances, ORR has also been the most commonly used surrogate endpoint in support of accelerated approval. Tumor response is widely accepted by oncologists in guiding cancer treatments. Because ORR is directly attributable to drug effect, single-arm trials conducted in patients with refractory tumors where no available therapy exists provide an accurate assessment of ORR.
rules.fda_notes: The 'reasonably likely to predict clinical benefit' standard for accelerated approval is less well-established than the 'known to predict clinical benefit' standard for traditional approval surrogates. Duration and depth of response are important considerations in evaluating ORR as a surrogate.
